pattern: The allowed moves let a coordinate pair reduce toward each other exactly like the Euclidean algorithm, so reachability comes down to whether their greatest common divisor is a power of two.
hints: The subtract-style moves behave like the steps of the Euclidean algorithm on the two coordinates, and the doubling moves only ever introduce factors of two.
Reduce the pair toward its greatest common divisor; a doubling move can be undone by dividing by two, so any leftover odd factor greater than one is unreachable.
The point is reachable exactly when the greatest common divisor of the two coordinates is a power of two (including one).

edge_inputs.description: coordinates whose gcd has an odd factor, unreachable
both coordinates at the minimum value
one coordinate at the minimum, the other at the maximum power of two below the bound
equal coordinates greater than one share exactly that value as their gcd
gcd is exactly a power of two larger than one
gcd is a power of two times a large odd cofactor on each side
both coordinates at the maximum allowed value

stress.description: coordinates constructed to share a power-of-two gcd
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    hi = max(1, min(size, 1000000000))
    power = 1 << rng.randint(0, 20)
    a = power * rng.randint(1, max(1, hi // power))
    b = power * rng.randint(1, max(1, hi // power))
    return [max(1, min(hi, a)), max(1, min(hi, b))]
